title: Structural, electronic, and optical properties of hexagonal GeSn from density functional theory
authors: Yetkin Pulcu, János Koltai, Andor Kormányos, Guido Burkard
abstract: Unlike cubic GeSn, which requires a high Sn concentration to undergo an indirect-to-direct bandgap transition, lonsdaleite (2H) germanium is an intrinsic direct-gap semiconductor. We employ first-principles density functional theory to investigate the structural, electronic, and optical properties of 2H-Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_{x}$ random alloys in the dilute Sn regime ($x \le 0.10$). The extended alloy disorder is modeled using 48-atom special quasirandom structure (SQS) supercells, and the coherent effective band structure is recovered via spectral band unfolding. We show that 2H-Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_{x}$ maintains a direct bandgap at the $Γ$ point across the studied composition range, exhibiting a strong bandgap bowing that shifts the fundamental absorption edge into the mid-infrared. Evaluation of the optical transition matrix elements reveals a giant polarization anisotropy dictated by spin-orbit coupling. The fundamental transition is strongly dipole-allowed for light polarized perpendicular to the crystal $c$-axis, an optical selection rule that is robustly preserved despite the random alloy disorder breaking the symmetry. These results demonstrate that hexagonal GeSn bypasses the compositional threshold limitations of the cubic phase, providing a highly tunable direct-gap system for infrared optoelectronics.
summary: This paper demonstrates that hexagonal GeSn alloys can achieve a direct bandgap at much lower tin concentrations than the standard cubic phase. This allows for efficient tuning of the material's absorption edge into the mid-infrared, making it a promising candidate for new infrared optoelectronic devices.
main_problem: The study aims to overcome the indirect bandgap and high Sn-concentration limitations of cubic GeSn by investigating the structural, electronic, and optical properties of the hexagonal (lonsdaleite) GeSn alloy phase for mid-infrared optoelectronics.
main_result: The 2H-Ge1-xSnx alloy maintains a direct bandgap at the Gamma point even at low Sn concentrations, exhibiting strong bandgap bowing into the mid-infrared and significant optical polarization anisotropy.
method: First-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the mBJ-LDA functional and spin-orbit coupling, employing Special Quasirandom Structure (SQS) supercells and band unfolding techniques.
model_or_system: The physical system is the hexagonal (2H/lonsdaleite) germanium-tin (Ge1-xSnx) random alloy in the dilute Sn regime (x <= 0.10), characterized by D6h crystal symmetry.
key_observables: Bandgap energy (Eg), lattice parameters (a, c), effective masses (in-plane vs. out-of-plane), absorption coefficient, and optical transition matrix elements.
important_parameters: Sn concentration (x <= 0.10), c/a ratio, and the mid-infrared wavelength range.
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the dilute Sn regime; optical properties are calculated in the independent-particle approximation without GW or BSE corrections; the alloy is modeled using finite-sized SQS supercells.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows atomic configurations and SQS supercells; Figure 2 plots lattice parameter evolution; Figure 3 compares electronic structures; Figure 4 shows effective band structure and spectral weight; Figure 5 shows bandgap evolution; Figure 6 illustrates polarization anisotropy of matrix elements; Figure 7 displays calculated optical absorption spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation for hexagonal GeSn, describes the DFT/SQS methodology, analyzes structural evolution, details electronic band structure and effective masses, evaluates optical properties and polarization, and concludes with a comparison to the cubic phase.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Atomic configurations of hexagonal group-IV sys- tems and random alloys. (a) Primitive cell of 2H-Ge and (b) the hypothetical 2H-Sn phase. (c)–(e) Representative 48- atom 3×2×2 Special Quasirandom Structure (SQS) supercells for 2H-Ge1−xSnx alloys at concentrations of x = 0.02, 0.04, and 0.06, respectively. Structures are viewed along the a-axis, and due to this projection, some atoms are located directly behind those in the foreground (e.g., larger Sn atoms visible behind Ge atoms).
Figure 2. Calculated lattice parameters a and c of 2H– Ge1−xSnx alloys as a function of Sn content x. The inset highlights the variation in the Ge-rich region (x < 0.12).

Figure 3. Comparison of the electronic band structures for pure 2H-Ge (gray lines) and the Ge0.9375Sn0.0625 alloy (blue scatter points) computed via the mBJ-LDA approach includ- ing SOC. The intensity of the blue color scale represents the projected spectral weight of the unfolded alloy bands, illus- trating how the direct gap at the Γ point is preserved despite the introduction of alloy disorder.

Figure 4. Unfolded electronic band structure of the hexago- nal Ge0.98Sn0.02 alloy (x ≈0.02) modeled using a 48-atom special quasirandom structure (SQS) supercell. The color scale denotes the spectral weight of the unfolded states. De- spite the presence of alloy disorder, the fundamental direct bandgap at the Γ point is preserved, with an extracted value of Eg = 0.246 eV.

Figure 5. Evolution of the fundamental band gap (Eg) as a function of Sn concentration. The plot compares various su- percell configurations to illustrate the transition from a semi- conducting to a semimetallic state (Eg = 0 eV). Band gap values were extracted from density of states calculations [48]. The linear fit for Vegard’s law (solid line) yields a slope of -30.7 ± 1.6 meV / at. %.
